## 1 Feedforward Network (FNN)

In a feedforward neural network, the input is propagated layer by layer through a sequence of linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions. The formulas below describe the feedforward computation using a combination of notations originally studied in Higham & Higham and Zemke. 

#### Windle (2026)

First, we define the layer notation for our network. The layers are noted as $l$ for layers $l = 1, \dots, L$ where $L$ is our final layer.

Next, lets define how the dimensions will be handled as we perform matrix multiplications at different layers. Will we use the vector 
$$
n_l \in \{n_0, n_1, n_2, \dots, n_L\}
$$ 
to denote the dimension. Here $n_1$ is the number of features for our sample $x$ and $n_0 = 1$. Also, $l$ is our layer with $L$ being the final layer. Simply, as we look at how our weights $W$, activations $a$ and bias $b$ compute, we will note that the dimensions for these follow a pattern as we move through the layers. This will hopefully become apparent in the example. 

We should now define the parts of our network.

The input layer of the feedforward network is as follows

$$
a^{[1]} = x,
\qquad
x =
\begin{bmatrix}
x_0\\
\vdots\\
x_k
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $k$ is the number of features from our input sample. Note that the $k$ number of features will also be our $n_1$ dimension. We also see that our $n_0 = 1$. This can be seens by the fact $x$ has a single column yet $k$ rows. This means that $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1 \times n_0}$ and that $a^{[1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1 \times n_0}$.

For layers $l = 2,\dots,L$, the feedforward update is

$$
a^{[l+1]}
=
\sigma\!\left(
W^{[l]} a^{[l]} + b^{[l]}
\right)
\tag{2}
$$

where $a^{[l+1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_0}$. Here we can see that no matter the layer, the activation $a$ is always an $n_l \times n_0$ matrix, where $l$ is the current layer dimension. This isn't exactly the dimension of the layer itself per say but it can be referenced by dimension. This will again be seen in the example.

The feedforward network can be defined as such:

$$
a^{[l+1]} = \sigma\!\left(z^{[l]}\right),
\qquad
z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} a^{[l]} + b^{[l]}.
\tag{3}
$$

Where $W^{[l]}$ is the weights and $b^{[l]}$ is our bias.

$$
W^{[l]} =
\begin{bmatrix}
w_{0,0} & \dots & w_{0, n_l} \\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
w_{n_{l+1}, 0} & \dots & w_{n_{l+1}, n_l}
\end{bmatrix},
\text{ where } W^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l+1} \times n_l}
$$

$$
\qquad
b^{[l]} =
\begin{bmatrix}
b_0 \\
\vdots \\
b_{n_0}
\end{bmatrix}
\text{ where } b^{[l]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l+1} \times n_0}
$$

where  
- $z^{[l]}$ is the pre-activation (linear transformation),  
- $a^{[l]}$ is the post-activation,  
- $\sigma$ is applied componentwise.

#### Example FNN

Layer 1: will have 3 input features. <br>
Layer 2: will have 4 neurons. <br>
Layer 3: will have 3 neurons. <br>
Layer 4: will have 1 output neuron. <br>

$$
a^{[1]} = x, 
\qquad
z^{[1]} = W^{[1]}a^{[1]} + b^{[1]}
$$

$$
x =
\begin{bmatrix}
x_0\\
x_1\\
x_2\\
x_3
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\text{where } x \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1 \times n_0}
\; (= \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 1}).
$$


$$
W^{[1]} =
\begin{bmatrix}
w_{0,0} & w_{0,1} & w_{0,2} & w_{0,3}\\
w_{1,0} & w_{1,1} & w_{1,2} & w_{1,3}\\
w_{2,0} & w_{2,1} & w_{2,2} & w_{2,3}
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\text{where } W^{[1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_2 \times n_1}
\; (= \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}).
$$


$$
b^{[1]} =
\begin{bmatrix}
b_0\\
b_1\\
b_2
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\text{where } b^{[1]} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_2 \times n_0}
\; (= \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}).
$$





---

#### Comparison

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; overflow-x: auto;"}
| **Concept** | **Higham** | **Zemke** |
|-------------|------------|-----------|
| Layer indexing | $\ell = 1,\dots,L$ | $i = 1,\dots,m-1$ |
| Input layer | $a^{[1]} = x$ | $a_1 = x$ |
| Preactivation | $z^{[\ell]} = W^{[\ell]} a^{[\ell-1]} + b^{[\ell]}$ | $z_i = W_i a_i + b_i$ |
| Activation output | $a^{[\ell]} = \sigma(z^{[\ell]})$ | $a_{i+1} = a_i(z_i)$ |
| Compact feedforward rule | $a^{[l]} = \sigma(W^{[l]} a^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]})$ | Same structure |
| Final output | $a^{[L]}$ | $a_m = f(x)$ |
| Componentwise | Yes | Yes |
:::




## 2 Cost Function

#### Higham & Higham (2018)

Higham defines the dataset cost function for the training set  
$(x^{(i)},\, y(x^{(i)})),\ i = 1,\ldots,n$ as

$$
\text{Cost}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\frac{1}{2}
\left\|
y(x^{(i)}) - a^{[L]}(x^{(i)})
\right\|_2^{2}.
\tag{9}
$$

where  
- $n$ is the sample size,  
- $a^{[L]}(x^{(i)})$ is the output of the network for sample $x^{(i)}$,  
- $y(x^{(i)})$ is the target vector,  
- $\|\cdot\|_2$ is the Euclidean norm.

---

For the backpropagation derivation, Higham uses the single-example cost

$$
C = \frac{1}{2}\,\|\, y - a^{[L]} \,\|_2^{2}.
\tag{18}
$$

---

#### Zemke (TUHH)

Zemke defines the full dataset cost as

$$
C(p)
=
\frac{1}{2N}
\sum_{j=1}^{N}
\left\|\, f(x_j) - y_j \,\right\|_2^2,
\tag{L02 S14) (2}
$$

where  
- $N$ is the sample size,  
- $f(x_j)$ is the network output for sample $x_j$,  
- $y_j$ is the target vector.

---

#### Zemke (TUHH)

For backpropagation, Zemke simplifies to the per-sample cost

$$
C
=
\frac{1}{2}
\,\left\|\, a_m - y \,\right\|_2^{2},
\tag{L2 S33) (3}
$$

where  
- $a_m$ is the output activation vector of the final layer,  
- $y$ is the target vector.

---

### Comparison

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; overflow-x: auto;"}
| **Concept** | **Higham** | **Zemke** |
|-------------|------------|-----------|
| Dataset cost | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac12 \|y(x^{(i)}) - a^{[L]}(x^{(i)})\|_2^2$ | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{j=1}^N \|f(x_j)-y_j\|_2^2$ |
| Single-sample cost | $\displaystyle C = \frac12\|y - a^{[L]}\|_2^2$ | $\displaystyle C = \frac12\|a_m - y\|_2^2$ |
| Output notation | $a^{[L]}$ | $a_m$ |
| Target notation | $y(x^{(i)})$ or $y$ | $y_j$ or $y$ |
| Treatment of outputs | Per-sample: $a^{[L]}(x^{(i)})$ | Whole vectors: $a_m$ |
| Norm | $\|\cdot\|_2$ | $\|\cdot\|_2$ |
:::

## 3 Backpropagation

Backpropagation computes the gradients of the cost function with respect to all
weights and biases in the network. Both Higham & Higham and Zemke introduce an
intermediate quantity, denoted by a lowercase delta, which measures how the cost
changes with respect to the pre-activation variables in each layer.

---

### Higham & Higham (2018)

We let the error term in layer $\ell$ be the vector

$$
\delta^{[\ell]}_j
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z^{[\ell]}_j},
\qquad
1 \le j \le n_\ell,
\qquad
2 \le \ell \le L,
\tag{21}
$$

where  
- $j$ is the neuron index within layer $\ell$,  
- $n_\ell$ is the total number of neurons in layer $\ell$,  
- $z^{[\ell]}$ is the pre-activation vector of layer $\ell$.

---

#### Lemma

$$
\begin{flalign}
\delta^{[L]}
&= \sigma'\!\left(z^{[L]}\right) \circ (a^{[L]} - y)
&&\tag{22}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\delta^{[\ell]}
&= \sigma'\!\left(z^{[\ell]}\right) \circ \left((W^{[\ell+1]})^T \delta^{[\ell+1]}\right)
&&\text{for } 2 \le \ell \le L-1 \tag{23}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial b^{[\ell]}_j}
&= \delta^{[\ell]}_j
&&\text{for } 2 \le \ell \le L \tag{24}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial w^{[\ell]}_{jk}}
&= \delta^{[\ell]}_j\, a^{[\ell-1]}_k
&&\text{for } 2 \le \ell \le L \tag{25}
\end{flalign}
$$

---

### Zemke (TUHH Lecture Slides)

Zemke uses the same lowercase delta symbol but indexes layers by subscripts. He defines, for layers $i = 1,\dots,m-1$, the error vector

$$
\delta_i
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_i}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_{i,1}} \\
\vdots \\
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_{i,n_i}}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\nabla_{x_i} C
\tag{L02 S33) (4}
$$

where  
- $z_i$ is the pre-activation vector of layer $i$,  
- $n_i$ is the number of neurons in layer $i$,  
- $C = \tfrac12 \|a_m - y\|_2^2$ is the per-sample cost.

---

### Lemma

$$
\begin{flalign}
\delta_{m-1}
&= \sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1})\,\circ\, (a_m - y)
&&\tag{L02 S33) (5}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\delta_i
&= \sigma_i'(z_i) \circ (W_{i+1}^{T}\delta_{i+1})
&&\text{for } i = m-1,\,m-2,\dots,1
\tag{L02 S33) (6}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_i}
&= \delta_i
&&\tag{L02 S34) (7}
\end{flalign}
$$

$$
\begin{flalign}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial W_i}
&= \delta_i\, a_i^{T}
&&\tag{L02 S34) (8}
\end{flalign}
$$



### Comparison

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; overflow-x: auto;"}

| Concept | Higham | Zemke |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Output-layer delta | $\delta^{[L]} = \sigma'(z^{[L]}) \circ (a^{[L]} - y)$ | $\delta_{m-1} = \sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1}) \circ (a_m - y)$ |
| Hidden-layer recursion | $\delta^{[\ell]} = \sigma'(z^{[\ell]}) \circ\left((W^{[\ell+1]})^T \delta^{[\ell+1]}\right)$ | $\delta_i = \sigma_i'(z_i) \circ (W_{i+1}^{T}\delta_{i+1})$ |
| Weight gradient | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial C}{\partial w^{[\ell]}_{jk}} = \delta^{[\ell]}_j\, a^{[\ell-1]}_k$ | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial C}{\partial W_i} = \delta_i a_i^{T}$ |
| Bias gradient | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial C}{\partial b^{[\ell]}_j} = \delta^{[\ell]}_j$ | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial C}{\partial b_i} = \delta_i$ |
| Indexing style | Superscripts $[\ell]$ | Subscripts $i$ |
| Activation derivative notation | $\sigma'(z^{[\ell]})$ | $\sigma_i'(z_i)$ |

:::

## 4 Haghmam Backpropagation Proof

We begin by proving (22). The relations (20) with $l = L$ shows that $z_j^{[L]}$ and $a_j^{[L]}$ are connected by $a^{[L]} = \sigma\left(z^{[L]}\right)$, and hence

$$
\frac{\partial a_j^{[L]}}{\partial z_j^{[L]}}
= \sigma'(z_j^{[L]}).
$$

Also, from (18),

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{[L]}}
= \frac{\partial}{\partial a_j^{[L]}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n_L} (y_k - a_k^{[L]})^2
= - (y_j - a_j^{[L]}).
$$

So, using the chain rule,

$$
\delta_j^{[L]}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{[L]}}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial a_j^{[L]}}
  \frac{\partial a_j^{[L]}}{\partial z_j^{[L]}}
= (a_j^{[L]} - y_j)\,\sigma'(z_j^{[L]}),
$$

which is the componentwise form of (22).

---

To show (23), we use the chain rule to convert from
$z_j^{[l]}$ to $\{z_k^{[l+1]}\}_{k=1}^{n_{l+1}}$.
Applying the chain rule and using the definition (21) where $j$ indexes a neuron in layer $l$ and $k$ indexes a neuron in layer $l+1$. This tells us which neuron $k$ in the current layer depends on neuron $j$ from the previous layer. Note: in the feed forward process we think of layer $l$ as being the current layer and layer $l+1$ as the next layer in the process. However, since we are working backwards for backpropagation, we will call $l+1$ the current layer and $l$ the previous layer.  

$$
\delta_j^{[l]}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n^{[l+1]}}
  \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_k^{[l+1]}}
  \frac{\partial z_k^{[l+1]}}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n^{[l+1]}}
  \delta_k^{[l+1]} \frac{\partial z_k^{[l+1]}}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}.
  \tag{26}
$$

Before continuing, let us recall from (19) that the weighted input to neuron $k$ in the current layer, $z_k^{[l+1]}$, and the weighted input to neuron $j$ in the previous layer, $z_j^{[l]}$, are connected via this feedforward pass

$$
z_k^{[l+1]}
= \sum_{s=1}^{n_l} w_{ks}^{[l+1]} \, \sigma\!\left(z_s^{[l]}\right)
  + b_k^{[l+1]}.
$$

Hence when looking at the partial derivative we see,

$$
\frac{\partial z_k^{[l+1]}}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
= w_{kj}^{[l+1]} \, \sigma'\!\left(z_j^{[l]}\right).
$$

In (26) this gives

$$
\delta_j^{[l]}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n^{[l+1]}}
  \delta_k^{[l+1]} \frac{\partial z_k^{[l+1]}}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n_{l+1}}
  \delta_k^{[l+1]} \, w_{kj}^{[l+1]} \, \sigma'\!\left(z_j^{[l]}\right),
$$

which may be rearranged as

$$
\delta_j^{[l]}
= \sigma'\!\left(z_j^{[l]}\right)
  \left( \left(W^{[l+1]}\right)^T \delta^{[l+1]} \right)_j.
$$
This is the component wise form of (23).

---

To show (24), we note from (19) and (20) that $z_j^{[l]}$ is connected to $b_j^{[l]}$ by

$$
z_j^{[l]}
= \left( W^{[l]} \, \sigma\!\left( z^{[l-1]} \right) \right)_j
  + b_j^{[l]}.
$$

Since $z^{[l-1]}$ does not depend on $b_j^{[l]}$, we find that

$$
\frac{\partial z_j^{[l]}}{\partial b_j^{[l]}} = 1.
$$

Then, from the chain rule,

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_j^{[l]}}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
  \frac{\partial z_j^{[l]}}{\partial b_j^{[l]}}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
= \delta_j^{[l]},
$$

using the definition (21).  This is the component wise form of (24).

---

Finally, to obtain (25) we start with the componentwise version of (19),

$$
z_j^{[l]}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n_{l-1}}
  w_{jk}^{[l]} \, a_k^{[l-1]}
  + b_j^{[l]}.
$$

where $j$ indexes a neuron in the current layer $l$ and $k$ indexes a neuron in the previous layer $l-1$.  

This gives

$$
\frac{\partial z_j^{[l]}}{\partial w_{jk}^{[l]}}
= a_k^{[l-1]},
\qquad\text{independently of } j,
\tag{27}
$$

and

$$
\frac{\partial z_s^{[l]}}{\partial w_{jk}^{[l]}}
= 0, \qquad \text{for } s \ne j.
\tag{28}
$$

In words, (27) and (28) follow because the $j$th neuron at layer $l$ uses the weights from only the $j$th row of $W^{[l]}$, and applies these weights linearly. Here $s$ is a dummy summation index used to run through all neurons $n$ in layer $l$. Then, from the chain rule, (27) and (28) give

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{jk}^{[l]}}
= \sum_{s=1}^{n_l}
  \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_s^{[l]}}
  \frac{\partial z_s^{[l]}}{\partial w_{jk}^{[l]}}
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_j^{[l]}}
  \frac{\partial z_j^{[l]}}{\partial w_{jk}^{[l]}}
= \delta_j^{[l]} \, a_k^{[l-1]},
$$

where the last step used the definition of $\delta_j^{[l]}$ in (21).  
This completes the proof.

:::

## 5 Zemke Backpropagation Proof

We begin by proving (5).  Relation (1) shows that $z_{m-1}$ and $a_m$ are connected by $a_m = \sigma_{m-1}(z_{m-1})$ and hence

$$
\frac{\partial a_m}{\partial z_{m-1}}
=
\sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1})
$$

Also, the derivative of the partial cost function with respect to $a_m$, interpreted
as a column vector (i.e., the gradient), is given by

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_m}
=
\frac{1}{2}
\frac{\partial \langle a_m - y,\; a_m - y \rangle}{\partial a_m}
=
\frac{1}{2}
\frac{\partial \left(a_m^{\top} a_m - 2y^{\top} a_m + y^{\top} y\right)}{\partial a_m}
=
a_m - y
=
\nabla_{a_m} C.
$$

By definition, the error vector in the final hidden layer is

$$
\delta_{m-1}
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_{m-1}}.
$$

Using the chain rule and the fact that $a_m = \sigma_{m-1}(z_{m-1})$, we obtain

$$
\delta_{m-1}
=
\frac{\partial a_m}{\partial z_{m-1}}
\,
\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_m}.
$$

Substituting the two derivatives computed above,

$$
\frac{\partial a_m}{\partial z_{m-1}}
= \sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1}),
\qquad
\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_m}
= a_m - y,
$$

we arrive at

$$
\delta_{m-1}
=
\sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1}) \circ (a_m - y).
$$

To understand the transposition due to the chain rule of the Jacobian Matrix, lets first review what is happening. For $i = 1,\dots,m-1$, since $a_{i+1} = \sigma_i(z_i)$ and $z_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{i+1}}$, the
Jacobian of the activation with respect to the pre-activation is

$$
\frac{\partial a_{i+1}}{\partial z_i}
\;:=\;
\left( J_{a_{i+1}}(z_i) \right)^{T}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\displaystyle \frac{\partial\, \sigma_{i}(z_1^{(i)})}{\partial z_{1}^{i}} 
    & \cdots 
    & \displaystyle \frac{\partial\, \sigma_{i}(z_{n_{i+1}}^{(i)})}{\partial z_{1}^{(i)}}
\\[1.2em]
\vdots 
    & \ddots 
    & \vdots
\\[1.2em]
\displaystyle \frac{\partial\, \sigma_{i}(z_1^{(i)})}{\partial z_{n_{i+1}}^{(i)}}
    & \cdots
    & \displaystyle \frac{\partial\, \sigma_{i}(z_{n_{i+1}}^{(i)})}{\partial z_{n_{i+1}}^{(i)}}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\operatorname{diag}\!\left( \sigma_i'(z_i) \right).
$$


Due to the (transposed) chain rule,

$$
\delta_{m-1}
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_{m-1}}
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial a_m}
\frac{\partial a_m}{\partial z_{m-1}}
=
\operatorname{diag}\!\left( \sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1}) \right)(a_m - y)
=
\sigma_{m-1}'(z_{m-1}) \circ (a_m - y),
$$

where $\circ$ denotes the componentwise multiplication (Hadamard product).

This proves equation (5).

---

To show (6) we first look at (1) where we have $z_{i+1} = W_{i+1} a_{i+1} + b_{i+1}$ for $i = 0,\dots,m-2$ and thus, we have

$$
\frac{\partial z_{i+1}}{\partial a_{i+1}}
= \bigl(J_{z_{i+1}}(a_{i+1})\bigr)^{\top}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\displaystyle \frac{\partial z^{(i+1)}_1}{\partial \sigma^{(i+1)}(z_1^{(i+1)})}
& \cdots &
\displaystyle \frac{\partial z^{(i+1)}_1}{\partial \sigma^{(i+1)}(z_{n_{i+1}}^{(i+1)})}
\\[1.1em]
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots
\\[1.1em]
\displaystyle \frac{\partial z^{(i+1)}_{n_{i+1}}}{\partial \sigma_i(z_1^{(i+1)})}
& \cdots &
\displaystyle \frac{\partial z^{(i+1)}_{n_{i+1}}}{\partial \sigma_i(z^{(i+1)}_{n_{i+1}})}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
w^{(i+1)}_{11} & \cdots & w^{(i+1)}_{1\,n_{i+1}} \\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
w^{(i+1)}_{n_{i+1}1} & \cdots & w^{(i+1)}_{n_{i+1}\,n_{i+1}}
\end{pmatrix}
= W_{i+1}^{\top}.
$$

```{tex}

```
Using the (transposed) chain rule, we obtain
$$
\delta_i
= \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_i}
= \frac{\partial a_{i+1}}{\partial z_i}
  \frac{\partial z_{i+1}}{\partial a_{i+1}}
  \frac{\partial C}{\partial z_{i+1}}
= \operatorname{diag}\!\bigl(\sigma_i'(z_i)\bigr)\, W_{i+1}^{\top} \delta_{i+1}.
$$

Finally, multiplication by the diagonal matrix
$\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_i'(z_i))$ is the same as
componentwise multiplication with the vector $\sigma_i'(z_i)$, so
$$
\delta_i
= \sigma_i'(z_i) \circ \bigl(W_{i+1}^{\top} \delta_{i+1}\bigr),
$$
which is exactly equation (6).

---

To show (7) and determine how to update the bias we begin with the equations for the derivative of $C$ with respect to $b_i$ where we again follow by using the (transposed) chain rule, since $z_i = W_i a_i + b_i$ and

$$
\frac{\partial z_i}{\partial b_i}
=
\bigl(J_{z_i}(b_i)\bigr)^{\top}
= I,
\quad\text{i.e.,}\quad
\frac{\partial C}{\partial b_i}
=
\frac{\partial z_i}{\partial b_i}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_i}
=
I \delta_i
=
\delta_i.
$$

---

Finally for (8), to compute the $\partial C / \partial W_i$, we drop the indices, i.e., we use
$\delta = \partial C / \partial z$ and $z = W a + b$.
The derivative of $z_i = e_i^{\top} z$ with respect to
$w_{i,j} = e_i^{\top} W e_j$ is given by

$$
\frac{\partial z_i}{\partial w_{i,j}}
=
\frac{\partial \bigl(\sum_k w_{i,k} \sigma_k + b_i\bigr)}{\partial w_{i,j}}
= \sigma_j = a^{\top} e_j,
$$

the other derivatives $\partial z_\ell / \partial w_{i,j}$, for $\ell \ne i$, are all zero.

Therefore the derivative of $C$ with respect to $w_{i,j}$ and the “matrix derivative”
$\partial C / \partial W$ are given by the one-dimensional chain rule as

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_{i,j}}
=
\frac{\partial C}{\partial z_i}
\frac{\partial z_i}{\partial w_{i,j}}
=
\delta_i \sigma_j
=
e_i^{\top} \delta a^{\top} e_j
\;\Rightarrow\;
\frac{\partial C}{\partial W}
= \delta a^{\top}.
$$

Substituting the missing layer index $i$ finishes the proof.
